item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excuse me what the heck. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 1 thing I don't understand: why, in the containment procedures, does it require one -1 instance to be kept alive on site?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He – he didn't respond so I called 112 on my mobile.

112 is primarily a European emergency number. In Australia, we'd call 000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fucked up, in a delicious way. And I like how unexplained it is, y'know? It's just this innocuous part of the planet that fucks you up in an absolutely horrid way for no reason and also it's forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the effects but, like others in this discussion session, I'm not really into the interview.

It's, frankly, redundant. It's not saying anything interesting or beyond what's described in the document, but harder to read because 2 thirds of the dialogue are terrified teens.

What I would like to see in its place:
- An exploration log that finds out or at least hints to what is in that cove that infects people with something that turns them into what I believe are roughly-human-shaped tunicates; for that matter, what is the nature of the infection(is it an actual disease, or just magic);
- A transcript of a victim's broadcasted thoughts. (If there are words to it; they could just be raw feeling, and that's fine; but if there are words, it could be more interesting)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. I feel that there is a lack of investigation on the Foundation's part. There's no mention of what is in the bay, or any attempts to investigate it or the water chemically. Since you aren't transformed immediately, I don't see why they wouldn't send in a D-class with a wetsuit and camera, or a robotic probe to look around. And yeah, the interview is flawed and might be unnecessary . I do like the detail from the fella about being indecisive, it kind of made the stuttering seem more in character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me a bit of "I have no mouth and I must scream."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This started out well, however, it doesn't really go anywhere much with the concept (Oddly enough, I might have upvoted if it ended after the description - would make for a fairly oldschool simple skip, and I'm a sucker for well done body horror. This also strikes me as a possible origin for my SCP-1308, but I digress ), the interview feels off, and it doesn't really tell me anything that I didn't already know.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the interview is excellent. You have a good grasp of the characters and thus their reactions to the situation.
I think it's be cool if you added another analysis or dialogue. That way the interview reads more as evidence in the file rather than an extraneous piece.
Great job overall! +

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The telepathic broadcast makes this really creepy, but that interview is really poor, all things considered. I honestly think you could drop it, it doesn't add a whole lot to the article that the article itself doesn't already give us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 because the idea of complete sensory deprivation and isolation has always creeped me out.

One quick thing though:

"And, um, Jack and Ethan convinced the others I'd be fun."

Did you mean to say "it'd"? Or was the "I'd" intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 because I was effectively grossed out to what happened to those poor kids. I wish you would revise the interview log so the rambly parts weren't so artistic with the spacing when they started panicking—it didn't seem like an authentic log; it read more like a diary entry. Otherwise, well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea, but god damn, the interview logs just kill it for me. The stuttering doesn't feel right at all. Also that random "Iwentintothewater" bit seems like the author tried really hard to insert something scary right there. Novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote for now. This fairly straight forward body horror. It is well executed but there is nothing to really hold my attention. With better interviews or logs I might find myself drawn into the narrative enough to about the people turning into telepathic lumps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good body horror, as it doesn't rely on gore. Nice potential for fridge horror, too, with the psychological horror aspect. I really like the telepathy bit.

I agree with others that the interview is the weakest point, but am on the fence as to whether or not I'd recommend cutting it entirely. I suppose Strunk and White would argue to cut that which doesn't definitely add.

Also the tenses switch around in the containment procedures, moving from a description of a fence that's apparently already there to a description of where monitors and guards "are to be placed."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this concept. I like the description of body horror.

However, the interview felt a bit off to me. When that one guy started panicking, I was halfway expecting him to turn into an instance of SCP-2051-1. I understand they both stutter a lot because they're under shock, but I think it would've been pulled off better if you provided a more vivid description of the pain the people felt when being converted (physically and emotionally).

Other than that, you got my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Take a look at my post.
He only waded in it, complete submersion is required for people to be infected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
with the cover story of accidentally swimming among Chironex flickeri.

You mean Chironex fleckeri.

IwentintothewaterIwentintothewaterIwentintothewater

I'm not sure why he's panicking. In fact, I'm not sure why you're using the cover story of jellyfish, because that specific type of jellyfish has an extremely painful and noticeable sting, and if we told the interviewees that's what made their friends melted, I don't think they would have bought it.

Wong and Rogers were given Class A amnestics released following containment of SCP-2051.

Didn't Rogers enter SCP-2051? Wouldn't he become SCP-2051-1 if he takes a bath or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Um, why they hell did they let Wong and Rogers go? Rogers obviously went into the water and he is therefore infected with this thing. The next time he takes a shower he'll turn into one of these blobs.

Edit: Nevermind, I just saw the parts about it needing complete submersion and that he only waded in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They do need to eat. If they don't, they start eating themselves, losing mass until there's nothing left.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually thought the interview was the weakest point, myself. The entry would be as good, if not better, if it was cut.

All in all, the article is well written, and the SCP itself is pretty darn creepy.

+1

EDIT - For clarity, I think the interview isn't needed because it doesn't need to be a people-focused story. The story of this SCP is it's squicky effects. The description does a good job of taking the reader through a little journey as the cove and its effects are described in escalating horror.

EDIT 2 - Of course, since you're getting only +1's so far, my opinion about the interview log is simply that. A humble, personal opinion :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Humans < telepathic prunes. Pruuuuuuuuuuuuuunes.

As funny as it is to me, I gotta ask - how/why do they autocannibalize, if they can't eat, have no organs, and no apparent need for sustenance? Why isn't the Foundation allowed to test this effect? Couldn't they use these stem cells?

I do like the interview, though. Hm.

E: Got it. I was being stupid and didn't really think about it. I'm still curious about the lack of testing, but the prunes are solid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks!

Also, I'd like to thank the many people in chat who put up with me begging for help. Dmatix, Djoric, Adeila, KaiserCrab, Nok_, Vivax, Piefish and Athena_Gray, I hope I got your names right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Despite a few awkwardly worded sentences, this is really good. The concept turned out to be far more disturbing than I expected.
